title: Who Is Telling Lies: Us or You?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which clear OCR character, punctuation, place-name, and word-break errors have been repaired. The two different MoU totals are preserved exactly where the source gives them. Reports and political claims remain attributed. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: At a campaign meeting in Coimbatore on 1 April, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa spoke about industrial development in her characteristic style. She accused me and Treasurer Thalapathi Stalin of falsely claiming that Tamil Nadu industrialists had gone to Karnataka. Yet in the same speech she admitted that Karnataka Chief Minister Siddaramaiah invited them to start industries at Chamarajanagar and that industrialists from Coimbatore district attended the meeting. Fortunately, she did not deny that. When the Chief Minister of Karnataka comes to a Tamil Nadu city and invites Tamil Nadu's industrialists to establish industries in his state, even an ordinary industrialist can understand what it means.

Dinamalar is not a DMK newspaper. On 22 January it published a report headed: “Tamil Nadu Pushes Away; Karnataka Attracts: Rs 12,000-Crore Investment Slips from Our Hands.” It said Rs 12,000 crore in investment by Kongu-region industrialists was set to move to Karnataka, principally because Tamil Nadu's investment climate had deteriorated. If this continued, industries elsewhere in Tamil Nadu might also migrate.

It reported that a 1,400-acre industrial zone was being developed in Chamarajanagar district on the border and that an investors' conference was held in Coimbatore on the twentieth. Siddaramaiah invited Tamil Nadu industrialists: “Come and start industries in Karnataka; we shall provide every facility.” About two hundred industrialists from Coimbatore, Tiruppur, Erode, Salem and the wider Kongu region reportedly came forward to invest Rs 12,000 crore at Chamarajanagar.
Neither Stalin nor I invented that conference. Dinamalar and other newspapers reported it. What meaning is there in Jayalalithaa going to Coimbatore and lamenting that we told a lie?

Karnataka's Chief Minister comes here to invite industries to his state. Should the Tamil Nadu government not be ashamed? If it cared about industrial development, should our Chief Minister not hold a conference in Bengaluru and invite Karnataka's industrialists here? Why attack us instead? We merely cited published reports.

After the conference, Karnataka Chamber of Commerce and Industry Assistant Secretary Vijayakumar reportedly said it had been a tremendous success and that offers exceeding Rs 12,000 crore in a single day were gratifying. Why did Jayalalithaa not deny it then?

Was it reported, or not, that a Tiruppur export company agreed to invest Rs 2,000 crore and a woman entrepreneur from Coimbatore expressed interest in investing Rs 1,000 crore? Why did she say nothing about this in Coimbatore?

Jayalalithaa instead claimed newspapers reported industrialists saying: “We were invited, so we came, that is all. Under no circumstances are we prepared to start industries at Chamarajanagar.” But Dinamalar actually reported some entrepreneurs saying they were not relocating existing industries but expanding there; that Karnataka suited granite polishing; and that food processing and wind-power expansion also had good prospects.

The newspaper added that even if these were additional investments, Tamil Nadu would have gained jobs had they come here. It said the investment climate was worsening because of power cuts, administrative failure, infrastructure shortages and the inability to meet rulers; entrepreneurs were moving to Chhattisgarh, Karnataka and Gujarat; and economists believed the Chamarajanagar conference could mark an important epoch in Kongu industrial history. The Chief Minister should have answered that newspaper. When we cite its report, how does that become false propaganda? Would those attending her meeting not have laughed?

On 24 January, Dinamalar devoted half a page to interviews about why investments were leaving Tamil Nadu. The headline itself explained the situation: “Bribery, Power Cuts and Land Prices Torment Tamil Nadu: This Is the Mystery Behind Western-District Investments Moving to Karnataka!” Instead of understanding such reports, what does it mean to tell an election meeting that we lie? When a headline says “Bribery Torments Tamil Nadu,” should the government not respond?

Rather than becoming angry with us, Jayalalithaa should read newspapers and understand what people think of her government, what the press writes and what is true. If one keeps flying in a helicopter, one cannot understand what happens below. She must learn to come down.

In Coimbatore, Jayalalithaa said her three-year government had signed MoUs with thirty-three Indian and multinational companies for Rs 31,306 crore. Can she say how many of those companies have actually begun operations in Tamil Nadu? Agreements may be signed with many; they help only when industries genuinely start.

At yesterday's meeting, she herself said that, although MoUs worth Rs 31,706 crore were signed over thirty-four months, only Rs 10,660 crore in investment had materialised. She had already spoken at length about these MoUs in the Assembly, and I gave a detailed reply on 6 February 2014. Because she did not read it, she repeated her Assembly speech in Coimbatore.
She says we lie about industrial development and investment leaving the state. Here is another example from her government's own industrial performance.
Under the DMK, 493,000 women worked in factories. Under the AIADMK, the number is 378,000. Thus 115,000 women lost employment. Is that an indication of industrial growth?

Another example: the State runs cement factories at Alangulam and Ariyalur. Alangulam produced 203,040 tonnes under the DMK in 2010-11, but only 134,640 tonnes under the AIADMK in 2011-12, a fall of 68,400 tonnes. Ariyalur produced 490,035 tonnes under the DMK and 388,055 tonnes under the AIADMK, a fall of 101,980 tonnes. Should production not rise when industry grows? Is this the sign of Tamil Nadu's industrial expansion?
And what has Subramanian Swamy, periodically so dear to Ammaiyar, said about her government? She may not have had time to read it, so I remind her:

“There is neither industrial nor economic prosperity. There is no need to divide Tamil Nadu and Karnataka, but I learn that Karnataka Chief Minister Siddaramaiah has attracted industrialists from here to his state. Rs 12,000 crore in industrial investment moved from Tamil Nadu to Karnataka because the Tamil Nadu government made no effort to retain it. Severe power shortages and non-cooperation from officials at every level mean no one from outside is willing to invest in Tamil Nadu. The expectation that Tamil Nadu would become India's foremost information-technology state has changed. The reason is the State government's poor administration.”
Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, who is telling lies: us or you?
With affection,
M.K.
3 April 2014
